description: Visualize the result of performance tests for Serverless

links.title: How to run tests and update the documentation

panels.description:
Real-time view of HTTP traffic during the test run.

Yellow line — request throughput in requests/second, calculated as a rolling rate over the last scrape interval.

Red line — percentage of failed requests. Spikes here indicate periods when the system started returning errors under load.

targets.expr:
sum by (testid, scenario) (
  irate(k6_http_reqs_total{testid=~"$testid", scenario=~"$scenario"}[$__rate_interval]) 
  and 
  present_over_time(k6_http_reqs_total{testid=~"$testid", scenario=~"$scenario"}[5s])
)

panels.title: Number of Requests / Failed Requests

panels.description:
Response time distribution for successful requests over the course of the test, overlaid with the number of active k6 Virtual Users (VUs).

Colored lines — response time percentiles.

Filled gray — number of active VUs on a logarithmic scale. Correlate VU ramp-up with response time growth to identify at what load level latency starts degrading.
A healthy system should show relatively flat percentile lines even as VU count increases.

targets.expr: sum(max_over_time(k6_vus{testid=~"$testid"}[5s]))

panels.title: Response Time (correct responses only) / k6 Virtual Users

targets.expr: sum(increase(k6_http_reqs_total{testid=~"$testid", scenario=~"$scenario"}[$__range]))
sum(increase(k6_http_reqs_total{testid=~"$testid", scenario=~"$scenario", expected_response="false"}[$__range]))

panels.description:
Summary of HTTP request performance per test run, scenario, runtime and resource profile. Each row represents a unique combination of these dimensions.

Response times (min, avg, med, p95, p99, max) are averaged across the test duration and show only successful responses. 

Rows with errors are highlighted in red — the errors column shows the total number of failed requests, and the total number of all requests sent during the test.

Use the testID and scenario filters above to narrow down the results.

To export the data as shown in this table, click the menu (three dots) in the top-right corner of the panel  → Inspect → Data → make sure "Apply panel transformations" is checked → Download CSV.
panels.title: Test Results Summary

targets.expr: scalar(max(kube_resourcequota{namespace="$NAMESPACE", cluster="$cluster", type="hard",resource="requests.cpu"}))
scalar(max(kube_resourcequota{namespace="$NAMESPACE", cluster="$cluster", type="hard",resource="limits.cpu"}))
scalar(max(kube_resourcequota{namespace="$NAMESPACE", cluster="$cluster", type="hard",resource="requests.memory"}))
scalar(max(kube_resourcequota{namespace="$NAMESPACE", cluster="$cluster", type="hard",resource="limits.memory"}))

list.description: Filter by test ID. Each test run has a unique ID assigned via k6 tag: k6 run --tag testid=<your-id>.
list.label: testID
list.name: testid

list.description: Filter by k6 scenario name. Scenarios are defined in the k6 script and represent different load scenarios.
list.name: scenario

title: Serverless Performance Tests
